subject: english
question: He said, 'I am writing a letter.'

options.A: He told that he was writing a letter.
options.B: He said that I was writing a letter.
options.C: He said that he was writing a letter.
options.D: He said that he is writing a letter.

explanation: When reporting a statement, 'am writing' (Present Cont.) changes to 'was writing' (Past Cont.). Option B is incorrect because it fails to change the tense.

subject: english
question: She said, 'I have finished my work.'

options.A: She told that she finished her work.
options.B: She said that I had finished my work.
options.C: She said that she has finished her work.
options.D: She said that she had finished her work.

explanation: Present Perfect ('have finished') changes to Past Perfect ('had finished'). Option B is wrong as it keeps the present perfect tense.

subject: english
question: Rahul said, 'I went to the market yesterday.'

options.A: Rahul said that he had gone to the market yesterday.
options.B: Rahul told that he had gone to the market yesterday.
options.C: Rahul said that he had gone to the market the previous day.
options.D: Rahul said that he went to the market the previous day.

explanation: Simple Past ('went') changes to Past Perfect ('had gone'), and 'yesterday' changes to 'the previous day'. Option D is wrong because it keeps 'yesterday'.

subject: english
question: Maya said, 'The sun rises in the east.'

options.A: Maya said that the sun rose in the east.
options.B: Maya said that the sun rises in the east.
options.C: Maya said that the sun had risen in the east.
options.D: Maya told that the sun rises in the east.

explanation: Universal truths do not change tense in indirect speech. Option B is incorrect because it changes the tense to simple past.

subject: english
question: He said, 'I will call you tomorrow.'

options.A: He said that he would call me the next day.
options.B: He said that he would call me tomorrow.
options.C: He told he would call me the next day.
options.D: He said that he will call me tomorrow.

explanation: 'Will' changes to 'would' and 'tomorrow' changes to 'the next day'. Option C is incorrect as it fails to change 'tomorrow'.

subject: english
question: She said, 'I can swim across the river.'

options.A: She said that she can swim across the river.
options.B: She said that she could swam across the river.
options.C: She said that she could swim across the river.
options.D: She told she could swim across the river.

explanation: 'Can' changes to 'could' in indirect speech. Option B is wrong because it leaves 'can' unchanged.

subject: english
question: The teacher said, 'Honesty is the best policy.'

options.A: The teacher said that honesty had been the best policy.
options.B: The teacher told that honesty is the best policy.
options.C: The teacher said that honesty was the best policy.
options.D: The teacher said that honesty is the best policy.

explanation: Proverbs and universal truths remain in the present tense. Option B is wrong because it changes the proverb to the past tense.

subject: english
question: He said, 'I am reading a book now.'

options.A: He told that he was reading a book then.
options.B: He said that he was reading a book then.
options.C: He said that he was reading a book now.
options.D: He said that he is reading a book then.

explanation: 'Am reading' changes to 'was reading', and 'now' changes to 'then'. Option B is incorrect because 'now' must change to 'then'.

subject: english
question: John said, 'I saw her yesterday.'

options.A: John said that he had seen her yesterday.
options.B: John told that he had seen her the previous day.
options.C: John said that he saw her the previous day.
options.D: John said that he had seen her the previous day.

explanation: Simple past 'saw' becomes past perfect 'had seen', and 'yesterday' becomes 'the previous day'. Option C is wrong because it keeps 'yesterday'.

subject: english
question: She said, 'I may attend the meeting.'

options.A: She said that she might attend the meeting.
options.B: She said that she may attend the meeting.
options.C: She said that she might attended the meeting.
options.D: She told she might attend the meeting.

explanation: 'May' changes to 'might'. Option B is wrong because it leaves 'may' unchanged.

subject: english
question: He said, 'My car was stolen.'

options.A: He said that his car had been stolen.
options.B: He said that his car was stolen.
options.C: He said that my car had been stolen.
options.D: He told that his car had been stolen.

explanation: Simple past passive 'was stolen' changes to past perfect passive 'had been stolen'. Option B is incorrect as it fails to shift the tense.

subject: english
question: They said, 'We were playing chess.'

options.A: They said that they were playing chess.
options.B: They said that we had been playing chess.
options.C: They told that they had been playing chess.
options.D: They said that they had been playing chess.

explanation: Past Continuous 'were playing' changes to Past Perfect Continuous 'had been playing'. Option B is incorrect because it does not change the tense.

subject: english
question: He said to me, 'Are you coming?'

options.A: He asked me if I was coming.
options.B: He asked me if was I coming.
options.C: He asked me that if I was coming.
options.D: He asked me whether I am coming.

explanation: For Yes/No questions, use 'if' or 'whether' and change to statement word order. Option B is incorrect because 'that' and 'if' cannot be used together.

subject: english
question: She said to him, 'Where do you live?'

options.A: She asked him that where he lived.
options.B: She asked him where did he live.
options.C: She asked him where does he live.
options.D: She asked him where he lived.

explanation: For Wh- questions, keep the question word and change to statement word order. Option B is wrong because it uses the interrogative order 'did he live'.

subject: english
question: Rahul said to me, 'Have you finished your work?'

options.A: Rahul asked me that if I had finished my work.
options.B: Rahul asked me if I had finished my work.
options.C: Rahul asked me if had I finished my work.
options.D: Rahul asked me if I have finished my work.

explanation: Present perfect changes to past perfect, and word order becomes a statement. Option B is wrong as it keeps the present perfect tense.

subject: english
question: The teacher said to the student, 'Why are you late?'

options.A: The teacher asked the student why was he late.
options.B: The teacher asked the student that why he was late.
options.C: The teacher asked the student why he was late.
options.D: The teacher asked the student why he is late.

explanation: Change interrogative order to assertive order ('why he was late'). Option B is incorrect because it retains the question order ('why was he').

subject: english
question: He said to her, 'Will you help me?'

options.A: He asked her that if she would help him.
options.B: He asked her if would she help him.
options.C: He asked her if she would help him.
options.D: He asked her if she will help him.

explanation: 'Will' changes to 'would', and pronoun 'you' changes to 'she'. Option B is incorrect because 'will' must change to 'would'.

subject: english
question: She asked, 'Can I borrow your pen?'

options.A: She asked that if she could borrow my pen.
options.B: She asked if could she borrow my pen.
options.C: She asked if she could borrow my pen.
options.D: She asked if I could borrow her pen.

explanation: 'Can' becomes 'could' and 'I' becomes 'she'. Option D is wrong because it uses interrogative word order.

subject: english
question: The policeman said to the man, 'What is your name?'

options.A: The policeman asked the man what was his name.
options.B: The policeman asked the man what his name is.
options.C: The policeman asked the man that what his name was.
options.D: The policeman asked the man what his name was.

explanation: The verb moves to the end in indirect wh-questions. Option B is wrong because it keeps the interrogative structure 'what was his name'.

subject: english
question: He said to me, 'Did you see the movie?'

options.A: He asked me if I had seen the movie.
options.B: He asked me whether I saw the movie.
options.C: He asked me if had I seen the movie.
options.D: He asked me if I saw the movie.

explanation: Simple past 'did see' changes to past perfect 'had seen'. Option B is incorrect because it keeps the simple past tense.

subject: english
question: My mother said, 'When will you return?'

options.A: My mother asked that when I would return.
options.B: My mother asked when I would return.
options.C: My mother asked when I will return.
options.D: My mother asked when would I return.

explanation: 'Will' changes to 'would', and word order becomes a statement. Option B is wrong due to interrogative word order.

subject: english
question: The manager said, 'Who broke this computer?'

options.A: The manager asked who broke this computer.
options.B: The manager asked who broke that computer.
options.C: The manager asked who had broken this computer.
options.D: The manager asked who had broken that computer.

explanation: Simple past 'broke' becomes past perfect 'had broken', and 'this' becomes 'that'. Option C is wrong because 'this' must change to 'that'.

subject: english
question: He said to the servant, 'Bring me a glass of water.'

options.A: He ordered the servant to brought him a glass of water.
options.B: He ordered the servant to bring him a glass of water.
options.C: He requested the servant to bring him a glass of water.
options.D: He ordered the servant that bring him a glass of water.

explanation: Commands use 'ordered/told to + V1'. Option C is wrong because 'requested' does not fit a command to a servant. Option B uses 'that'.

subject: english
question: The doctor said to the patient, 'Take your medicine regularly.'

options.A: The doctor advised the patient taking his medicine regularly.
options.B: The doctor ordered the patient to take his medicine regularly.
options.C: The doctor advised the patient to take his medicine regularly.
options.D: The doctor advised the patient that take his medicine regularly.

explanation: Medical advice uses 'advised to + V1'. Option C is wrong because 'advised taking' is incorrect grammar here.

subject: english
question: The teacher said to the boys, 'Do not make a noise.'

options.A: The teacher ordered the boys to not make a noise.
options.B: The teacher forbade the boys to make a noise.
options.C: The teacher advised the boys not to make a noise.
options.D: The teacher forbade the boys not to make a noise.

explanation: When using 'forbade', do not use 'not' as 'forbade' is already negative. Option B is wrong because it creates a double negative.

subject: english
question: She said to me, 'Please help me with this bag.'

options.A: She ordered me to help her with that bag.
options.B: She requested me that help her with that bag.
options.C: She requested me to help me with that bag.
options.D: She requested me to help her with that bag.

explanation: 'Please' indicates a request, and 'this' becomes 'that'. Option B is wrong because pronouns are not changed correctly.

subject: english
question: The captain said to the soldiers, 'March forward!'

options.A: The captain commanded the soldiers to march forward.
options.B: The captain commanded the soldiers to marched forward.
options.C: The captain commanded the soldiers that march forward.
options.D: The captain requested the soldiers to march forward.

explanation: Military orders use 'commanded to + V1'. Option D is wrong because the infinitive verb 'to march' must be in base form.

subject: english
question: He said to the child, 'Do not play with fire.'

options.A: He warned the child not to play with fire.
options.B: He warned the child to not play with fire.
options.C: He warned the child that do not play with fire.
options.D: He ordered the child not playing with fire.

explanation: Negative commands use 'warned/told not to + V1'. Option D is wrong because 'that do not play' is grammatically incorrect.

subject: english
question: My father said to me, 'Work hard for your exams.'

options.A: My father advised me to worked hard for my exams.
options.B: My father advised me to work hard for my exams.
options.C: My father advised me that work hard for my exams.
options.D: My father ordered me to work hard for my exams.

explanation: Parental guidance is typically reported as advice. Option D is incorrect because infinitives use the base verb 'to work'.

subject: english
question: The officer said, 'Shoot the prisoner!'

options.A: The officer requested to shoot the prisoner.
options.B: The officer ordered to shoot the prisoner.
options.C: The officer ordered shooting the prisoner.
options.D: The officer ordered that shoot the prisoner.

explanation: Strong commands use 'ordered to'. Option C is incorrect as 'requested' contradicts the severity of the command.

subject: english
question: He said, 'Alas! I am undone.'

options.A: He told with sorrow that he was undone.
options.B: He exclaimed with sorrow that I am undone.
options.C: He exclaimed with joy that he was undone.
options.D: He exclaimed with sorrow that he was undone.

explanation: 'Alas' indicates sorrow. The tense changes to past. Option D is wrong because 'told with sorrow' is incorrect reporting verb usage.

subject: english
question: She said, 'Hurrah! We have won the match.'

options.A: She exclaimed with joy that we have won the match.
options.B: She exclaimed with sorrow that they had won the match.
options.C: She exclaimed with joy that they had won the match.
options.D: She exclaimed with joy that they won the match.

explanation: 'Hurrah' indicates joy. Present perfect changes to past perfect. Option C is wrong because it uses simple past.

subject: english
question: He said, 'What a beautiful flower it is!'

options.A: He exclaimed that what a beautiful flower it was.
options.B: He exclaimed with wonder that it was a very beautiful flower.
options.C: He asked what a beautiful flower it was.
options.D: He exclaimed with wonder that it is a very beautiful flower.

explanation: Exclamatory sentences with 'what' or 'how' become statements with 'very' or 'great'. Option C is wrong as it keeps the present tense.

subject: english
question: The old man said, 'May you live long!'

options.A: The old man prayed that I might live long.
options.B: The old man prayed that I may live long.
options.C: The old man asked that I might live long.
options.D: The old man told that I might live long.

explanation: Optative sentences use 'prayed' or 'wished', and 'may' becomes 'might'. Option B is wrong because it keeps 'may'.

subject: english
question: She said, 'Bravo! You have done well.'

options.A: She applauded him that he had done well.
options.B: She told him bravo that he had done well.
options.C: She applauded him, saying that he had done well.
options.D: She exclaimed with joy that he has done well.

explanation: 'Bravo' is reported using 'applauded... saying that'. Option B is wrong as it keeps the present perfect tense.

subject: english
question: He asked me where I was going.

options.A: He said to me, 'Where are you going?'
options.B: He said to me, 'Where you are going?'
options.C: He said to me, 'Where I am going?'
options.D: He said to me, 'Where was I going?'

explanation: Reverse conversion requires changing past continuous back to present continuous interrogative. Option D is wrong due to assertive word order.

subject: english
question: She told him that she would come the next day.

options.A: She said to him, 'I will come the next day.'
options.B: She said to him, 'She will come tomorrow.'
options.C: She said to him, 'I would come tomorrow.'
options.D: She said to him, 'I will come tomorrow.'

explanation: 'would' becomes 'will' and 'the next day' becomes 'tomorrow'. Option C is wrong because it retains 'the next day'.

subject: english
question: They exclaimed with joy that they had won.

options.A: They said, 'Alas! We have won.'
options.B: They said, 'Hurrah! They had won.'
options.C: They said, 'Hurrah! We have won.'
options.D: They said, 'Hurrah! We won.'

explanation: Past perfect reverts to present perfect, and 'exclaimed with joy' reverts to 'Hurrah!'. Option D is wrong because simple past is less accurate here.

subject: english
question: The doctor advised the patient to quit smoking.

options.A: The doctor asked the patient, 'Do you quit smoking?'
options.B: The doctor said to the patient, 'Quit smoking.'
options.C: The doctor ordered the patient, 'To quit smoking.'
options.D: The doctor said to the patient, 'You quit smoking.'

explanation: An imperative sentence in direct speech becomes an infinitive in indirect. Option B is wrong because imperatives drop the subject pronoun.

subject: english
question: He said that the sun is a star.

options.A: He said, 'The sun is a star.'
options.B: He said, 'The sun was a star.'
options.C: He said that, 'The sun is a star.'
options.D: He told, 'The sun is a star.'

explanation: Universal truths remain identical in direct speech. Option D is wrong because 'told' cannot be used without an object.
